slides.heading: Meet Kavya 👩‍💻
slides.body:
Let's start with a story.

Kavya is 22 years old. She recently discovered she loves coding. She's been learning for about two months — watching tutorials, building small things, breaking things, figuring things out.

Three weeks ago, she started her biggest project yet: a simple to-do app.

Nothing fancy. Just a web page where you can:
  → Add a task
  → Check it off as done
  → Delete it when you're finished

But it's *hers*. She wrestled with every bug. She stayed up late getting the checkboxes to work. She chose the colours herself.

Today, it's working. And it feels great.
bullets: 💻 2 months into learning to code
📄 One file: todo.js — everything she's built
✅ Working app — add, check, delete tasks
😊 She's proud of it. And she wants to make it even better.

slides.heading: "I'll add dark mode. How hard can it be?" 🌙
slides.body:
It starts with a small idea.

Kavya uses her app late at night. The white background is bright in the dark. A few people she showed it to mentioned it too.

"I'll add a dark mode toggle. Just a button that switches the colours. Easy."

So she opens her file — her one todo.js — and starts editing.

She changes the background colour. She changes the text colour. She adjusts the button styles. She digs into parts of the code she hasn't touched in weeks.

An hour passes. Then two. She's so deep into it now, she barely notices.

"This is actually coming together," she thinks.
bullets: 🌙 Idea: dark mode toggle for night-time use
📂 Opens todo.js — the one file with everything
✏️ Two hours of editing — changing colours, styles, layout
🤔 She's been modifying code she hasn't touched in weeks...

slides.heading: Disaster — the moment everything breaks 💥
slides.body:
Two hours in. Kavya opens her browser to check the result.

The page loads.

The checkboxes? Gone. Just... gone.

The font is wrong — it's using some browser default she never set. The delete buttons are misaligned. And a strange error is flickering in the console.

She stares at the screen, confused. She only changed the *colours*. How did the checkboxes stop working?

Something she edited — somewhere — broke something else. That's how code works. Everything is connected.

She thinks: no problem. I'll just undo it.
bullets: 🖥️ Opens the browser to check her progress
❌ Checkboxes gone — completely missing from the page
❌ Font broken, buttons misaligned, console errors
🤔 She only changed colours... but everything is connected in code

slides.heading: Undo... undo... undo... too far 😭
slides.body:
Kavya opens her editor. Ctrl+Z. Ctrl+Z. Ctrl+Z.

The editor undoes her changes.

But here's the problem: she made hundreds of small edits over two hours. The undo function steps back through *every single one*.

She's watching her code change in real time. The dark mode colours disappear. But then so do the delete buttons. Then the checkbox logic. Then the styles she spent four days perfecting.

She goes too far.

She presses Ctrl+Z one too many times and now the file is *empty*.

All of it — the working todo app she spent three weeks building — gone.

She saves. She closes the file. She opens it again hoping it was a dream.

Blank file. `// empty`.

She starts crying.
bullets: ⏪ Starts pressing Ctrl+Z — undoing her changes
📉 Each undo steps back through hundreds of edits from two hours of work
💀 Goes too far — undo deletes the original working code too
😭 Blank file. Three weeks of work. Gone.

slides.heading: Kavya rebuilds — and makes a new rule 🧠
slides.body:
After sitting with it for a while, Kavya does what any determined person does: she rebuilds.

From memory. From screenshots. From what she can remember.

It takes four days to get back to where she was.

But she makes herself a promise: "Before I change *anything*, I will make a backup copy first."

So she starts doing that. Before every change, she duplicates the file and gives it a name.

At first it feels great! She has a safety net. It's working.

But then two weeks pass.
bullets: 💪 Spends 4 days rebuilding the app from memory
🤝 Makes a promise: always make a copy before changing anything
📂 Starts naming and saving backup files manually
😌 For a while, it feels like the right solution...

slides.heading: Two weeks later — the folder of doom 🗂️
slides.body:
Kavya needs to go back to the version that had the animations working. She added them last week, then something changed and they vanished.

She opens her project folder.

She has... seven files.

Tap each file below. Can you tell which one has the working animations?

(Kavya can't either. She spent 45 minutes trying. She never found it.)

The backup system she invented solved ONE problem — she can go back. But it created TWO new ones:

  ▸ She can't tell what's in each file without opening it
  ▸ She's terrified to delete any of them in case she needs one
bullets: 🕐 45 minutes of digging through old zip files
🤷 None of the file names say what's actually inside
😰 Seven files. No idea which one is the right one.
→ Tap each file below — see if the names help you figure it out

slides.heading: A friend wants to help — sounds good, right? 🤝
slides.body:
Kavya's friend Rohan is also learning to code. He sees her todo app and offers to help.

"I can add a filter feature — like 'show only incomplete tasks'. It'll be great!" he says.

Kavya thinks: two people working on this is twice as fast!

So they split the work:
  → Kavya takes the search bar feature
  → Rohan takes the filter buttons

They both download the latest version of todo.js.
They both open their editors.
They both start coding.

Separately.

On the same file.

With no way to communicate their changes to each other.

Can you see where this is going?
bullets: 👩‍💻 Kavya: coding the search bar feature
👦 Rohan: coding the filter buttons feature
📂 Both editing their own copy of the same file
⏱️ Both working for 3 hours separately...

slides.heading: The merge disaster 💥
slides.body:
Three hours later.

Rohan is done first. He emails Kavya his todo.js file with the filter buttons.

Kavya gets the email. She sees the attachment. She copies his file into the project folder.

Click. Replace.

Her version is gone. All three hours of her search bar code — replaced by Rohan's file.

He didn't do anything wrong. She didn't do anything wrong. There was just no way for two people to safely work on the same file and combine their work afterward.

Rohan's version only has filters.
Kavya's version only had search.
Now there's no version with both.

She stares at the screen. Rohan stares at the screen. Neither of them knows what to do.
bullets: 📧 Rohan sends his version via email
📋 Kavya copies it into the folder — replacing hers
💥 Her 3 hours of search code: gone. Only Rohan's filters remain.
😶 Nobody made a mistake. The system just doesn't work for two people.

slides.heading: Three problems. Every developer faces them. 🤔
slides.body:
What happened to Kavya isn't unusual.

Every person who has ever coded without version control has hit these exact walls.

Think about what each of these problems *actually* needed:

  Problem 1: She needed a way to take a "photo" of her working code that she could always go back to — without touching the original.

  Problem 2: She needed photos that came with clear labels — "working todo app with checkboxes" — not just a filename.

  Problem 3: She needed a system where two people could save their own photos and then intelligently combine them.

These aren't complicated ideas. Humans have been doing versions of all three for centuries.

In the next step, we'll discover those ideas — before we even open a terminal.
bullets: Problem 1 → Need: take a photo of working code, go back to it anytime
Problem 2 → Need: photos with clear labels that explain what's inside
Problem 3 → Need: combine two people's photos without destroying either's work
→ Next: discover the solutions — through examples you already understand
